text: Look at this, that is a human embryo implanting itself, caught in 3D for the first time ever. You remember Eggmeat's sperm to make embryo. But then, that embryo attaches or implants itself into the lining of the uterus where it gets the nutrients to grow. But studies tell us this only goes right about 40% of the time, making it one of the biggest roadblocks to reproduction. But we don't really know why, because it's hard to study what's happening inside a pregnant person. To get a better look, scientists created a gel that mimics the uterus and set up a 3D microscope to capture footage, and watched how a mouse embryo and a human embryo latched on. Healthy embryos pull strongly. This one even collapses itself and plunges in. Scientists found that mouse embryos only pull in a couple of directions and stay mostly on the surface of the gel, while the human embryo pulled all over the place and sunk deep into the gel. We reached out to the scientists and they told us that thanks to this work, they've already developed a new protein supplement that improves implantation by almost twofold. So if you believe there should be more optimistic science and tech stories, follow up.
